name: SERS Interlaboratory Adenine Dataset (Solid Gold)
description: Quantitative SERS spectra of adenine measured using solid gold substrates across 15 different European laboratories. Benchmarks model reproducibility and inter-instrumental variability.

distribution.name: SERS Interlaboratory Adenine Dataset (Solid Gold)
distribution.description: Source data hosted at the original repository.

field.name: spectrum_id
field.description: Sequential integer index identifying each spectrum.

field.name: raman_shift_cm_inv
field.description: Raman shift wavenumber axis in cm⁻¹. Shape: (n_wavenumbers,).

field.name: spectrum_intensity
field.description: Raman scattering intensity in arbitrary units. Shape: (n_wavenumbers,) per spectrum.

field.name: target
field.description: Target value(s): concentration, physical property, or other continuous measurement.

rai:dataCollection: SERS spectra of adenine in 0.01 M pH 7.4 phosphate buffer acquired on solid gold substrate (gold-coated silicon nanopillar arrays, Silmeco ApS, Denmark) at 785 nm excitation. Collected as part of the first European multi-instrument SERS interlaboratory study (ILS) within EU COST Action BM1401 Raman4Clinics, involving 15 laboratories from 11 European countries and 44 researchers (July-October 2018). Six instrument models from three manufacturers were used across labs (WiTec Alpha 300R, Horiba LabRAM 300, Horiba LabRAM ARAMIS, Horiba LabRAM HR 800, Horiba T64000, Renishaw InVia); laser power and acquisition time were set by each lab within prescribed limits (10x or 20x magnification). Three different substrates and three spectra per substrate were collected per sample, yielding nine spectra per concentration level per method per laboratory. The analyte concentration range spans 0 to approximately 4 µM at five levels.
rai:dataPreprocessingProtocol: Raw spectra preprocessed following a centrally defined Data Analysis Protocol (DAP) implemented in R (version 3.4.3, packages hyperSpec and dplyr). Steps include extended multiplicative scatter correction (EMSC), smoothing, spectral range selection, baseline correction, and normalization. Quantification performed by integrating the SERS ring-breathing band area between 715 and 750 cm⁻¹ and fitting a linear inverse least-squares calibration model. Calibration curves with poor linear fit (r² < 0.6 or F-test p > 0.01) were excluded from the final analysis.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Ground-truth adenine concentrations were gravimetrically prepared by the organizing laboratory (University of Trieste) as standard solutions in 0.01 M pH 7.4 phosphate buffer, then aliquoted and shipped to all participants. A blind design was applied - only the organizing laboratory knew the test set concentrations. Calibration models were built by inverse least-squares regression on integrated SERS band areas of the adenine ring-breathing mode (715-750 cm⁻¹).
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information is included. All spectra originate from adenine reference solutions without individual human identifiers.
rai:dataBiases: Data collected with a single solid substrate type (commercially available gold-coated silicon nanopillars, Silmeco ApS, Denmark) and a single analyte (adenine); results are specific to this substrate-analyte combination and should not be extrapolated to other SERS substrates or analytes. Only 785 nm excitation was used for the solid gold method (sAu@785); no green-laser coverage is included. Signal variability arises from differences in laser power, optics, and detector responsivities across participating instruments (six models from three manufacturers). The producer's recommended measurement (full substrate map) was replaced by three spectra from random locations per substrate due to practical constraints, which may reduce spatial averaging of signal heterogeneity. Only five of eight expected laboratory datasets reached the final analysis.
rai:dataLimitations: Results valid only within the studied concentration range (0-4 µM) and for the specific solid gold substrate; extrapolation to other concentrations or substrates is not validated. RMSEP 28% (SEP 29%, BIAS -2%) represents the highest variability among the four substrate methods in this study, indicating lower inter-laboratory reproducibility for this substrate. The three-spectra-per-substrate sampling replaces the producer-recommended full map and may under-represent spatial heterogeneity of the substrate. Only five of eight expected laboratory datasets are included in the final analysis. Intrinsic SERS signal variability (substrate-to-substrate fabrication differences, hot spot distribution) limits quantitative reproducibility.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Represents the first large-scale European collaborative benchmark for quantitative SERS, contributing to the standardisation of SERS measurement protocols across laboratories. Supports the development of reproducible, validated SERS methods for clinical diagnostics, food safety, and environmental monitoring within the Raman4Clinics initiative (EU COST Action BM1401).
rai:dataUseCases: Benchmarking quantitative SERS calibration methods on solid gold substrates across instruments and laboratories; evaluating inter-laboratory reproducibility and trueness; development and testing of SERS spectral preprocessing and regression algorithms; comparing solid gold versus colloidal and solid silver SERS substrates for quantitative analytical applications.
prov:wasGeneratedBy: Data collection: Laboratory measurements on chemical samples including pure compounds, mixtures, and solutions. Preprocessing: Spectra may have undergone baseline correction, smoothing, normalisation, and wavenumber calibration. Exact steps are described in the accompanying publication. Annotation: Ground-truth target values measured by validated reference analytical methods (e.g., enzymatic assays, dynamic light scattering, gas chromatography, or DFT computation).
